data.title: Mach Natural Resources: Wait For A Full Business Cycle Record
data.description:
Mach Natural Resources LP (MNR) is an upstream partnership that issues a K-1. Typically, this combination does not work well throughout the business cycle. Back when I first began writing for Seeking Alpha, there were various income models that several upstream companies had set up (including K-1s). None of those models are around now.

The reason for this seems to be the big oil price decline that occurred in 2015 followed by the COVID challenges in 2020. The two of those events seem to have proven that the idea did not work. It should also be pointed out that 2008 was no picnic for this type of setup either.

Upstream is very dependent upon commodity prices. Low commodity prices do offer the potential of reported income on a K-1 without getting a distribution. That means that you have to pay taxes without getting the cash to do so. K-1 partnerships do much better with a midstream setup where the income is much steadier (and reliable) with some downside protection.

The other issue is the calculation of disposable cash is fixed. This often allows for a generous distribution during a time when commodity prices are elevated (such as oil prices right now). But the company is reporting a debt ratio of 1.4 currently. There is a target of a debt ratio of 1.0. The problem with this in the past has been that a significant downturn turned a comfortable debt ratio into something nearly impossible to service. This is where a lot of these kinds of K-1s vanished for any number of reasons.

This company, on the other hand, went public in 2023.

Since going public, the stock has clearly dropped about 50% from its high point and rebounded a bit on the Iranian situation that has raised commodity prices. However, most K-1s are buying considerations at market bottoms when commodity prices are weak. The current situation is the polar opposite of a buy consideration. This is probably the time to consider selling because “the sun is shining and the forecast is for sunny skies well into the future.” Except that this is a low visibility industry that can quickly change environments with little warning.

The issue for me is that the debt is not the priority that the distribution to unit holders is. In my opinion, what should be happening right now is a paydown of the debt (as fast as possible) to a very low debt ratio so that the debt can still be properly serviced if the worst happens. It is noted in the 10-K that debt can become a priority. But usually when that happens, it is too late. There is insufficient cash flow at that time (usually a cyclical downturn near the bottom) to deal with the situation. If the entity survives in any form, usually there is a lot of dilution and a complete change to a corporation going forward.

The last issue is that this entity is operating in some interesting basins that are generally not considered low cost even though they are viable basins. There is every chance the seller wanted out of the properties because the seller had gone through the latest natural gas pricing downturn (and had enough).

Areas Of Operation

Probably the San Juan Basin serves as a good example:

The San Juan Basin Royalty Trust (SJT) has struggled for some time. It was a basin that had a fairly long period of inactivity before the trust reported drilling again on the expectation of stronger natural gas prices. But the trust has also not issued a distribution for some time because it has considerable loans (and debts) to repay. In fact, there is a serious possibility that it may have to liquidate because it is getting near the end of its financial rope.

Because Mach formed after a lot of struggles in the basin, it likely purchased the properties cheap from a lot of anxious sellers, similar to the situation with the San Juan Basin Royalty Trust. Right now, the situation looks really good. But this industry is cyclical. There will likely be more downcycles in the future. Oftentimes, these downcycles give little notice of the severity until it is really too late.

Risk

This is why it is highly recommended to watch something like this through a full business cycle before investing. Some of these turn out to have costs that are too high to make it through a downcycle with really any debt at all. Others pay out money that should have been used to repay debt, and then a downcycle hits (and they are gone for one reason or another).

Right now, the distribution is enticing, and cash flow looks good with the Iranian crisis. But no one knows how long the current situation will last. A wrong guess could prove to be very damaging to your portfolio or even fatal (because these are things that happened in the past).

The other consideration is that the low decline rate in this era of unconventional wells often means that the production is older. But that also means that lease operating costs will rise as production declines. Now there is some drilling activity that may keep costs down. But this is yet another good reason to wait for a full business cycle of results before investing.

Patience in this business is worth money. That is especially true for all of us who were writing about this type of setup back around 2015 only to see it fall apart over the next several years. Learn from all of us that got the “bumps and bruises” to show for this idea.

Summary

Mach Natural Resources LP is a K-1. Such a setup in the past has been found to have serious defects. In fact, many of those defects resulted in K-1s in the upstream business disappearing. That never stops others from trying (as is the case here). But since there have been so many failures in the past, it might well be best to wait to see if this particular setup makes it through a cyclical downturn (and in what condition).

This industry is very fast moving, volatile, and low visibility. What looks good when prices are reasonably high can quickly become untenable during a cyclical downturn. Because of that low visibility, cyclical downturns rarely advertise the severity and duration until it is too late.

For me, that makes this a sell consideration. I generally want a five-year history, at least, for any new issue before I consider it. In this industry, I prefer a full business cycle unless I know the management. With a K-1 model, as is the case here, I want to be especially careful before I consider investing. This setup does not have a good upstream track record.

data.title: State Of The Pipeline - March 2026
data.description:
This feature has the latest news from the mutual bank conversion sector. As of March 25, five conversions were in the pipeline.

Carteret, New Jersey-based URSB Bancorp Inc.'s (URSB) mutual-to-stock conversion offering priced at the supermax. The shares are expected to begin trading March 27.

According to the prospectus, many of the one- to four-family, multifamily, commercial and industrial (C&I), and consumer loans at URSB Bancorp unit United Roosevelt Savings Bank have been purchased rather than originated internally. As of Sept. 30, 2025, the balance of purchased C&I loans from BHG Financial, formerly known as Bankers Healthcare Group LLC, was $35.3 million, or 12.5% of the bank's loan portfolio.

United Roosevelt began buying C&I loans from BHG in early 2016. The bank also started buying participation interests in syndicated leveraged lending loans in November 2023 from BancAlliance Inc.; the balance of that exposure on Sept. 30, 2025, was $4.0 million. Additionally, United Roosevelt began purchasing consumer loans from BHG and Woodside Credit LLC in the second quarter of 2025 and from LendingClub Corp. (LC) in the first quarter of 2025.

On March 13, CSB Financial Inc. (CSB:PVT), the proposed holding company for Bethel, Ohio-based Community Savings Bank, filed a registration statement for a mutual-to-stock conversion. Community Savings began buying consumer loans from BHG in the second quarter of 2024. The consumer loan portfolio, virtually all of which came from BHG, comprised 18.7% of the bank's total loans as of year-end 2025. In the filing, the bank said, "We intend to continue to purchase unsecured consumer loans." The bank also disclosed that it has purchased C&I loans from BHG and that the majority of its one- to four-family loans are secured by nonowner-occupied properties.

Also on March 13, Poughkeepsie, New York-based Rhinebeck Bancorp Inc. (RBKB) filed a registration statement for a second-stage conversion. The exchange ratio for the newly issued shares is between 1.0367 at the minimum of the offering range and 1.4026 at the maximum. There is no supermax level. At the end of 2025, nonowner-occupied commercial real estate accounted for 27.3% of the bank's loan portfolio and indirect auto represented another 22.3%.

In the filing, Rhinebeck Bancorp said, "While we still plan to originate indirect automobile loans, over the past four years and for the foreseeable future we have actively decreased our indirect automobile portfolio by decreasing loan originations through increased pricing and more selective underwriting criteria."

Rhinebeck Bancorp also noted that it no longer originates residential mortgage loans directly and doesn't offer construction or rehabilitation loans for one- to four-family properties.

PSB Financial Inc. (PSB:PVT) has filed a prospectus for a mutual-to-stock conversion. PSB is the proposed holding company for Pioneer State Bank, the successor to Deer Lodge, Montana-based Pioneer Federal Savings and Loan Association. The price to pro forma tangible book value as of Dec. 31, 2025, was between 41.5% at the minimum of the offering range and 53.7% at the supermax. The closing date for the offering is April 21.

In the filing, PSB said, "We are considering branching opportunities that may arise in our primary market area, including a potential branch office in western Montana in late 2026 or the first six months of 2027."

On Feb. 2, Fair Lawn, New Jersey-based Columbia Financial Inc. announced a relatively rare type of transaction: a mutual bank conversion plus a bank acquisition. Columbia's announcement was for a second-stage conversion and the simultaneous acquisition of Woodbridge, New Jersey-based Northfield Bancorp Inc.

Then on March 6, Columbia filed its conversion registration statement. The exchange ratio ranges from 1.8729 at the adjusted minimum of the offering range to 2.534 at the maximum. As with Rhinebeck Bancorp, there is no supermax. Following the transaction, Columbia intends to pay a quarterly cash dividend of 5 cents per share.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: GitLab: Growth Is Slowing, But Cheap Enough To Keep Buying
data.description:
As we move deeper into 2026, selling pressure continues to build against software stocks. Investors who were used to having upward multiples re-ratings drive the majority of stock gains over the past few years are now seeing the unfortunate inverse. And because it’s difficult to ascertain the impacts of AI on sales, we may be looking at a multi-quarter or even multi-year rut in the sector.

Amid these conditions, however, investors with long time horizons have a great chance to buy high-quality businesses at a sharp discount. GitLab (GTLB) is one of these stocks. The DevOps software platform, which helps companies build and deploy software applications, has seen a ~40% reduction in its share price since the start of the year and a ~60% loss in the past twelve months. The question for investors now is, will the bleeding continue, or is it time to step in?

I last wrote a buy article on GitLab in December, when the stock was trading at $40 per share. I fully acknowledge that the timing of my buy call was ill-timed, and needless to say, I wasn’t able to predict that the “SaaSpocalypse” narrative would overtake markets in 2026 and sink GitLab further. That said, with a decline of this magnitude, it helps to take a step back and look at the stock with fresh eyes. Yes, it’s true that GitLab’s growth is decelerating. But there is little evidence to substantiate the notion that AI will entirely kill its business. With that in mind, I’m reiterating my buy call on this stock.

Let’s start with the elephant in the room that has tanked GitLab’s stock: its guidance for FY27. For the year ending in January 2027, as shown in the chart below, GitLab has guided to $1.10-$1.12 billion in revenue, which reflects 15-17% y/y growth. This represents a deceleration from 23% growth in Q3, and unfortunately does little to defray the fear that AI is killing off GitLab’s market. Further, the company is expecting pro forma operating income to fall -18% y/y, with pro forma operating margins decaying to 12% relative to a 17% margin in FY26.

We won’t argue that these metrics aren’t a concern. GitLab has a lot of work ahead of it to reset its sales traction and continue expanding its customer base. Yet there are glimmers of hope: the company notes that recent new logo adds have rebounded. It’s possible that guidance is setting the stage for a low bar to cross, with beat-and-raise quarters ahead of us in FY27.

The upshot here that we need to consider is that this weakness is already priced into GitLab’s stock. At current share prices near $21, GitLab trades at a market cap of $3.46 billion. After we net off the $1.26 billion of cash (against zero debt) on GitLab’s most recent balance sheet, the company’s resulting enterprise value is $2.20 billion.

This represents a 2.0x EV/FY27 revenue multiple. It’s true here that we need to take GitLab’s execution on a bit of faith: investors are demanding that software stocks be reasonably valued against the bottom line, and with the company expecting operating margin erosion this year, it’s difficult to take that for granted. That said, we shouldn’t ignore the fact that GitLab trades well below most software companies that are growing at a similar mid-teens pace:

While we think it may take several quarters for investors to get comfortable with GitLab again (we'll need to see proof of strong execution and GitLab maintaining at least mid/high teens revenue growth), the company should be able to recover its multiples closer in line with peers: at least in a 3-4x revenue multiple range.

Furthermore, consider the nature of the AI “risk” for GitLab. It’s true that vibe coding and AI may reduce the need for software engineers whose principal job is to write code. But we’re far away from a future (and perhaps will never achieve) in which AI generates usable, shippable code in the first iteration without the need for human review, revision, and ultimately support and maintenance. It is platforms like GitLab that enable this type of work: so as long as the company can decouple its business model from seat-based licenses (which it already has done) and base it on consumption, GitLab is likely never to become obsolete.

Stay long here and use the dip as a buying opportunity.

Q4 download

Let’s now go through GitLab's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

GitLab's revenue grew 23% y/y to $260.4 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $252.2 million. Revenue growth decelerated two points from 25% y/y growth in Q3.

Again, we do think that GitLab's growth slowdown is worth noting. That said, it's not all bad news for the company either. GitLab grew its total base customer count to 10,682, adding 207 net-new customers within the quarter. Moreover, the count of customers generating >$1 million in ARR grew 26% y/y to 155 customers.

We wouldn't be seeing this type of customer activity if GitLab was a platform at near-term risk of being decommissioned. The fact that larger enterprise customers are committing to heavy spending with GitLab is a healthy signal of the company's durability.

And we note that while management has cited disappointment with recent sales trends, its recent trend of logo additions has been on the rise. The company has also added to its salesforce, which may drive a near-term hit to margins but sets the company up for growth recovery as tenures improve. Per CEO Bill Staples' remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

FY '27 is all about execution and proving our hypothesis with results. Let me walk through each one. In FY '26, we reversed a long period of first order deceleration. This is critically important because our customers often land small but extend steadily, a pattern that's held for over a decade. Sales-led first orders began reaccelerating in Q2 FY '26 right after Ian joined. On the product side, Manav has reinvigorated product-led growth.

First order logos inflected in October, and we've seen 4 consecutive months of improvement. For FY '27, we now see a clear path to sustained acceleration in first orders, driven by continued sign-up momentum, new product-led on-ramps and a dedicated first order sales team with a new global leader, 4 regional leads in place and rapid hiring underway. In fact, the team has already closed their first deals in Q1 [...]

With regard to sales capacity, we began increasing headcount in FY '26, and we're entering FY '27 with more capacity than we've ever had. And we have a path to even stronger ramped capacity beginning in Q3. With FY '27 kickoff, we've overhauled territory design to also better serve all segments and strengthen enablement."

Management noted as well that gross retention is consistent with historical trends, while churn has hit a 4-year low: all additional signals that AI isn't driving any near-term crisis for GitLab.

For now, profitability is also expanding. Pro forma operating margins rose 3 points y/y to 21%:

The company's guidance calling for margin erosion in FY27 is a reflection of the company's recent step-up in sales hiring, but if new logo additions continue to outpace expectations, we expect the company to be able to "beat and raise" above its current bar.

Risks and key takeaways

It's true that GitLab has faced an elevated amount of execution risk lately. We should be cognizant of the risks that sit on the near-term horizon for the company. As a weak macro persists and puts pressure on IT budgets, we have evidence of companies putting less budget priority on DevOps tools like GitLab while devoting IT spending to projects that directly enable AI. We note as well that GitLab operates in a competitive environment, and it has a number of notable direct competitors like JFrog (FROG) that has seen better recent sales execution.

At the same time, it's far too premature to call out AI as an existential threat to GitLab. It's still growing its large customer counts, is growing revenue at a >20% pace, and at least as of Q4 still retained a "Rule of 40" growth/profitability balance. Stay long here and use the dip as a buying opportunity.

data.title: Hermès: In An AI World Flooded With Abundance, Scarcity May Become Even More Valuable
data.description:
I wrote about Hermès (HESAY) (HESAF) previously. At the time, I saw it as a wonderful brand, even a model for luxury brands, carrying fantastic financial performance. Unfortunately, the valuation seemed off at the time, and at $235 per share, I thought it only deserved a Hold rating, and I even added that I would consider it at $190. The gentleman below made a very important note:

If you are waiting for a stock of this caliber to trade cheap, you are in for a hard time. And he was right; only two years later has the stock hit the level I had defined, and I must say it was more luck than merit.

In my view, the only reason the brand reached this level again is mainly due to an awful macro tape, where the closing of the Strait of Hormuz is akin to a tax on global productivity, which makes Hermès goods relatively less valuable (declining output of the rest of the economy), and the market is just discounting it. But in the end I believe the story is still about manufactured scarcity and the AI productivity boom that ultimately strengthens, not weakens, the Hermès thesis.

Recent performance

The recent selloff in the stock seems more macro than micro, given that the company has entered 2026 from a strong position. In 2025, the company recorded close to $18 billion in revenue, with an operating margin of 41.75%. It should be noted that this is a very strong combination in any consumer category, let alone a year marked by tariff uncertainty and currency pressure.

The balance sheet is also in great shape with a current ratio of almost 5 and a debt-to-assets ratio below 10%, confirming modest debt usage and plenty of liquidity available for the company.

The leather goods and saddlery are still the most important segment for the company’s business, and they grew at 13.1% in 2025. The cash generation is impressive, with the company recording close to $5 billion in 2025.

This gives the companies multiple options, although the path ahead seems to be the continuation of the cautious strategy of reinvesting in the controlled scarcity model through the slow ramp of new workshops in a way that won’t dilute the brand while retaining ample liquidity to cushion macro cycles without having to resort to layoffs. This last point is key given that the company needs a lot of time to train new artisans, and it makes no sense to train them to let them go because of cyclical fluctuation in business.

All-in-all, if the closing of the Strait of Hormuz is indeed the catalyst for a global downturn, Hermès appears to be in a good position to cushion this without having to resort to short-term measures and eventually throw the proverbial baby out with the bathwater.

Valuation

A look into valuation allows us to confirm that Hermès is indeed a different breed. Even with the recent drawdown, the stock remains a clear outlier on sales multiple. At 11x sales, it sits well above all the other companies. The market continues to price Hermès as a scarcity asset with fantastic economics.

This actually supports our assumption that the drawdown was macro-driven, since the premium has remained. The market decided to mark down the stock since the “global tax on global productivity” squeezes the surplus that finances discretionary spending. But, still, it wasn’t willing to value the company as an ordinary luxury company.

On the P/E multiple, the story is similar, even if there are some nuances. For starters, the sales multiple seems much more extreme than the PE. Usually that happens when the market is not just rewarding current profit margins but the whole structure of the business: we mean the brand power, the scarcity manufacturing model, direct distribution, and production bottleneck. All these features mean that future growth might be available at the expense of no luxury brand dilution.

Risks

There are a couple of risks that can attack the earnings, multiple or the scarcity thesis. Let’s review them. The present macro shock can compress the multiple. As we have framed it, this is a global tax on productivity that trickles down, reducing the surplus for high-end discretionary spending. Also, in these situations the market can become indiscriminate, and everything gets sold at the same time. The stock can go down without the business breaking.

China and Asia represented 42% of the revenue, and it is by far the biggest revenue exposure. The current political tensions or even problems in the Chinese economy can turn into a problem for Hermès.

Probably the most important thesis-breaking risk would be if production volume suddenly starts to outrun the consumer perception of the brand’s scarcity. The whole idea in this company is to scale without becoming common. If there are troubles, the first warning will come from the resale market losing steam. That alone can trigger a violent re-rating.

Wrapping up

Hermès is a rare publicly traded scarcity asset. The recent drawdown is likely motivated by macro worries that compress the global surplus from which discretionary spending (including luxury spending) is funded. Nevertheless, the long term revenue engine remains intact with $18 billion in revenues in 2025, and a 41% operating margin, providing the clout to advance the scaling of scarcity without cheapening it. In a world on the cusp of being flooded with synthetic abundance from AI, human-made capacity constrained physical objects may become relatively more valuable, not less.

An interesting way to wrap this up is to build a bull and bear scenario. For the bull scenario, let’s consider the revenue estimates for 2028, with the same multiple and constant share count. In that case the equity value would be 11.2 times $25.16 billion (high end of analysts estimates), or $282 billion. Considering 105 million ordinary shares (10 ADR for 1 ordinary share in Euronext Paris Exchange), means a target of $268.

For the bear scenario, let’s consider the low end of the 2028 estimates, $22.86 billion at 7.1x revenue (Ferrari multiple), which results in an implied equity value of $162 billion, or $154 per share. Given the current price of $188, the bull case implies an annual return of close to 13%, while the bear case provides a return of -6%.

All in all, the risk/return relation is positive even if the overall potential seems limited. I loved the business model back in 2024, and I love the business even more today. The stock price dipping is a gift, not a problem, in my perspective. I think it deserves a place in any long term-oriented investor, therefore I am rating it a buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Gilead Sciences: Caution After A Re-Rating Amidst New Concentration Risks
data.description:
Shares of Gilead Sciences (GILD) have been on a major run higher in recent years, far outperforming many peers that have been struggling. This made sense, as Gilead traded at very low valuations from the get-go.

The problem is that this move higher has been driven by modest growth, yet amidst a re-rating in the valuation and Gilead rapidly getting reliant on its HIV business, I am much more cautious here. This compares to a much more bullish outlook in the fall of 2024. A recent M&A streak is not going to change that cautious stance here.

Other, higher conviction ideas, including recent IPOs, can be found at Value In Corporate Events.

Continued M&A

Gilead announced its second >$1 billion deal before the first quarter of the year had ended. Towards the end of March, Gilead announced a $1.675 billion cash deal to acquire Ouro Medicines, a privately held biotechnology company that focuses on developing T cell engager therapies for autoimmune diseases.

With this deal, the company will get its hands on OM366 (gamgertamig), which has shown great efficacy in phase 1/2 clinical studies in antibody-mediated orphan diseases, with the FDA having granted fast-track and orphan drug designation. The deal involves another half a billion in contingent milestone payments, but all this could change a bit as Gilead is in discussions with Galapagos to effectively "share" the deal, as both firms could pay up 50% in this transaction.

About a month ago, Gilead announced a much larger $7.8 billion deal to acquire Arcellx (ACLX) in a $115 per share cash deal, excluding a potential $5 per share earn-out through a contingent value consideration. Arcellx has multiple drugs in its pipeline; the most promising is a CAR T-cell therapy for multiple myeloma.

With the company spending up to $10 billion in M&A, that was not all, as the company hiked the dividend in February. Following a modest near 4% increase in the payout, these payouts now come in at $0.82 per share each quarter, at an expense of about $4 billion per year.

On A Stronger Footing

Gilead announced solid 2025 results early in February. Reported revenues rose 2% to $29.4 billion. However, underlying growth came in at 4%, with regular product revenue reported at $28.0 billion; that is, if we exclude Veklury (the COVID-19 drug of Gilead), whose revenues were cut in half to less than a billion in 2025.

The problem, or challenge, for Gilead is that the company is creating a reliance issue again. The HIV business grew sales by 6% to $20.8 billion, making up nearly three-quarters of product revenue. Liver disease revenues grew 6% to $3.2 billion, this once being a cornerstone of the initial wave of success of Gilead, which now has been stabilized. Oncology sales were actually down 2% to $3.2 billion, with other revenues coming in below the billion mark.

Earnings are quite lumpy, with adjusted earnings of $8.15 per share for 2025 being nearly twice the number a year before, with a substantial part of the volatility being the result of volatile IPR&D impairment charges, depressing 2024's earnings to a large degree.

Paying for the recent M&A streak is relatively easy, as a net debt load of $17 billion and change has come down to $14 billion over the past year. While this will jump to $24 billion on a pro forma basis, such debt levels are very manageable for a firm the size and profitability of Gilead here. This comes as the 2025 guidance calls for a continuation of modest growth, with product sales excluding Veklury seen up just over a billion to $29.0-$29.4 billion. Adjusted earnings are seen at a midpoint of $8.65 per share, up half a dollar from the year before.

With 1.25 billion shares outstanding trading at $137 per share, the company commands a market value of $171 billion and a $185 billion enterprise valuation ahead of the recent deals. This values the business at just over 6 times sales, and based on the adjusted earnings guidance, valuations come in at around 17 times trailing earnings and 16 times forward earnings.

This marks a huge re-rating for a stock that traded around 10 times earnings many times in recent years. Such observation comes even after shares are down twenty dollars from the highs a couple of weeks ago, as shares have still doubled from a $70 mark in the summer of 2024.

What Now?

While the re-rating has been substantial and adjusted earnings in the case of pharmaceutical companies can be quite adjusted, I fear the composition of growth of Gilead. After all, following a transformative acquisition of Pharmasset, Gilead became a hugely successful business, thriving on its HCV business in the 2010s. Note that Gilead at large is still reporting sales way short compared to the sales peak around that time, induced by the HCV boom.

With the company rapidly curing these patients, the fortunes of the business turned as the company was in desperate need of new blockbusters. To that end, the company used a lot of capital to pursue M&A, on average to relatively little avail. This included a $21 billion deal to acquire Immunomedics, a $12 billion deal for Kite Pharma, a $4 billion deal for CymaBay, multi-billion investments in Galapagos, all ahead of recent M&A efforts here and smaller deals announced meanwhile.

Meanwhile, absent some volatility induced by the COVID-19 drug Veklury, the share of HIV has been increasing rapidly as the HCV business imploded. That was not all, as HIV grew while other segments were seen coming down, despite their M&A efforts. All of this makes it so that the share of HIV product sales has risen to nearly three-quarters of product sales here, making me very cautious in between more demanding valuations and more concentrated risks.

Having held a position in Gilead for a long period of time, I collected relatively fat dividends as I have been gradually trimming the position on the move higher. By now, I have sold out of the last shares around the $130 mark.

This decision was driven by re-rating and the dependency risks created here, and while the company has seen shares come down a bit recently, that follows strong momentum to the upside before as well. However, their recent M&A efforts are encouraging, but frankly, it is very difficult to see how these deals will pan out and how product revenues will evolve, as that remains wait-and-see. Growth in such acquired, or internally developed drugs is key to changing the narrative here, as the future will tell if, how fast, and how large this will happen.

That will be key, as seeing appeal at such multiples requires more growth and more diversified growth, with few signs seen at this point, making it easy for me to avoid the shares here.

data.title: Why Active Bond Management Matters When Spreads Are Tight
data.description:
Corporate bonds delivered a strong 2025. The broad investment-grade market posted a total return of approximately 7.77%, rewarding investors who maintained fixed income allocations through a volatile rate environment.1 More than $540 billion flowed into taxable bond funds over the course of the year, the highest annual total on record.2

That enthusiasm, however, has compressed credit spreads to levels not seen in decades. Investment-grade option-adjusted spreads entered 2026 in the 2nd percentile of their 20-year range, leaving little room for further tightening.3 The question facing bond investors now is straightforward: in an environment where the easy gains from spread compression may already be behind us, where does income come from next?

Tight Spreads Demand a Different Approach

When spreads are wide, nearly any diversified bond portfolio benefits from the rising tide. When spreads are historically tight, the margin for error shrinks. A single credit misstep can erase months of carry. This is the environment bond investors face in 2026.

Analysts at Aberdeen Investments have noted that security selection becomes more important than simply owning the market when spreads offer limited compensation for credit risk.3

Charles Schwab has echoed a cautiously constructive view, calling 2026 “another generally good year for bonds” while emphasizing the importance of selectivity.4 Corporate bond supply may reach a record $2.25 trillion in gross issuance this year, which means investors will face no shortage of new paper to evaluate.5

Investment-grade credit spreads have compressed steadily since the 2022 peak and now sit near the tightest levels observed in two decades, reducing the passive beta opportunity in corporate bonds. Basis Points (bps). Beta - in finance measures an investment’s volatility or systematic risk compared to the overall market (usually the S&P 500), acting as a gauge for sensitivity.

An Active Approach to Corporate Bond Income

The Infrastructure Capital Bond Income ETF (BNDS) is built for precisely this kind of market. Launched in January 2025, the fund is actively managed and holds a focused portfolio of approximately 73 corporate bonds selected through a combination of quantitative and qualitative analysis.6 The management team emphasizes securities it believes are undervalued on the basis of term premium, credit premium, and liquidity premium.

BNDS currently offers a 30-day SEC yield of 7.67%, well above the yields available from broad market benchmarks such as iShares Core US Aggregate Bond ETF (AGG) or Vanguard Total Bond Market Index Fund ETF (BND).6 Monthly distributions of $0.34 per share, recently increased in December 2025, underscore the fund’s income-first mandate.

Top holdings reflect a preference for mid-tier corporate credits in energy infrastructure and real estate, including names like Genesis Energy, Chemours, Plains All American Pipeline, Sunoco LP, and BW Real Estate.6 This is not a fund that mirrors the index. The concentrated, conviction-driven positioning is a deliberate feature of the active strategy. *Fund holdings are subject to change at any time and should not be considered a recommendation to buy or sell any security. Click here for the fund’s Top Ten Holdings.

Taxable bond fund inflows surged past $540 billion in 2025, with active strategies capturing a growing share of total flows. Two-thirds of bond ETFs created since 2019 are actively managed. Data obtained from Morningstar.

What This Means for Income Investors

The broader shift toward active fixed income management is well documented. Active bond ETFs captured more than $100 billion in flows through September 2025 alone, representing roughly 40% of all fixed income ETF inflows.2 That trend reflects a growing recognition that index-hugging strategies may leave income on the table, particularly when the spread environment offers limited passive tailwinds.

With the Federal Reserve holding the fed funds rate at 3.50%–3.75% and markets pricing further easing toward 3.00%–3.25% over the course of 2026, the backdrop for bond returns remains constructive.7

The opportunity, though, increasingly lies in careful credit work and disciplined security selection rather than broad market exposure. Funds like BNDS, with an expense ratio of 0.81% and a transparent, income-focused mandate, represent one way investors can pursue that opportunity.6

Tight spreads do not necessarily mean poor returns. They mean the returns must come from somewhere other than passive spread compression. Active management, selective credit exposure, and income enhancement strategies offer a path forward for investors willing to look beyond the index.

Current yield comparison

BNDS offers a 30-day yield of 7.67% as of 12/31/2025, significantly above the yields available from broad bond benchmarks (AGG, BND) and the 10-year Treasury. Performance data quoted represents past performance and is no guarantee of future results. Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. For the most recent month-end performance, please call (800) 617-0004 or visit the fund's website at https://www.infracapfund.com/BNDS.

Notes

1. Breckinridge Capital Advisors, Q1 2026 Corporate Bond Market Outlook, January 2026.

2. Morningstar, 8 Charts on U.S. Fund Flows in 2025, January 2026.

3. Breckinridge Capital Advisors, Q1 2026 Corporate Bond Market Outlook, January 2026; Aberdeen Investments, Global Fixed Income Outlook 2026, December 2025.

4. Charles Schwab, The Bond Market in 2026: What Could Go Wrong?, January 2026.

5. Morgan Stanley, 2026 U.S. Credit Strategy Outlook, November 2025.

6. Infrastructure Capital Advisors, BNDS Fund Overview and Prospectus, 2025.

7. Federal Reserve Board, FOMC Statement, January 28, 2026.

data.title: Bank of America: Not A Bad Time To Buy The Dip
data.description:
Last fall, I reiterated my buy rating on Bank of America Corporation (BAC). With resilient business activity seen across all their segments, profitability having improved, and the valuation at attractive levels, I maintained a bullish view on the stock. Since then, declines have been deeper than for the S&P 500, and so there has been underperformance that I did not expect. While the firm did report their latest earnings quite a while back, I have decided to provide an update now to see where things may go next as we head into their Q1 report in April.

In the analysis below, it is shown that Q4 was generally a good quarter for Bank of America, as activity and profitability looked good. Moving forward, guidance is respectable, and opportunities generally outweigh the risks right now. Early Q1 numbers are sounding bullish, and with the valuation having pulled back significantly to start 2026, I have decided to maintain my buy rating on the stock.

A Recap Of 2025 Q4

Let's begin today with a review of some of their results for Q4 since that should give us a good starting point. In the fourth quarter, Bank of America generated revenues net of interest expense of $28.4 billion, or $28.5 billion in FTE terms. This represented a growth rate of 7% YoY, and while that is a slowdown from Q3 it still shows they have been resilient. One of the largest sources of strength was net interest income. As shown in the chart on the right, NII grew by 10% to show that this rate environment has remained quite favorable for a large part of their business. In the year-ago quarter, NII growth was just 3%, and so if rates go mainly unchanged this year, Bank of America could be set for further strength in this key metric. At this point, CME FedWatch shows that rates are most likely to remain unchanged from current levels at the 2026 year-end.

As an indication of broad strength in their business, all four of their core segments saw revenues increase YoY. Some highlights included robust equities trading revenues that were up 23% and higher asset management fees that increased by 13%. While investment banking fees saw quite weak growth, investors should recall that we had a government shutdown that delayed IPOs in the quarter. As a whole, I consider Q4 to have been a respectable quarter in terms of activity, and that is also conveyed by the fact that they beat revenue expectations by $624.74 million.

In terms of how efficient the firm has been, there have been notable improvements seen in their 2025 Q4. As shown above, total non-interest expense increased by just 3.6%. This is notably slower than the revenue growth rate to show that expenses were well controlled. Another good sign that efficiency has improved is, of course, the YoY 194 bps decline in the efficiency ratio. Lastly, these improvements translated into robust profitability as both ROE and ROTCE expanded YoY. As a whole, investors should rest easy that Bank of America continues to be increasingly well run.

Financial Outlook

Bank of America does provide financial guidance, so let's see what they are expecting moving forward. For NII, they see around 7% growth in Q1 which would be a dip from what we saw in Q4. As for the full year, the midpoint growth rate of 6% would also be a slight dip from the 7% we saw in 2025. Therefore, despite a good likelihood that rates will stay in a favorable position for the firm, it seems that activity may slow moving forward with the macro environment continuing to be uncertain. As for business efficiency, non-interest expense is expected to increase by just 4% in Q1 while 200 bps of operating leverage is expected for the full year. To me, this suggests that Bank of America will continue to be well run moving forward.

Private Credit Could Be A Double-Edged Sword

In the financial sector, the hottest topic is, without a doubt, the crisis of confidence in the private credit space. With some asset management firms facing withdrawals and markdowns, financial stocks as a whole have come under some notable pressure. For Bank of America, this may mark an opportunity and a risk moving forward.

Earlier this month, a report came out that stated Goldman Sachs (GS), JPMorgan (JPM), and Bank of America are offering products that allow for hedge funds to bet against the private credit market. With there being a potential for a 2008-style crash, it is not surprising that such products are in demand right now, and for these banks, it seems they are monetizing this crisis. This means for Q1 and beyond, Bank of America may see a windfall from this strategic move.

While this crisis may be an opportunity, it should also be noted that the firm has its own capital in private credit as well. It was reported back in February that they would be putting $25 billion of their own cash towards private credit. At this point, it is unclear if they entered risky deals or whether they have picked up bargain assets amid the fear. Nonetheless, with weak confidence in this area likely to persist, I would say that investors should also monitor for signs of stress.

Asset Quality Pressures?

For Bank of America, you can see on the right that their net charge-off ratio has inched lower in recent times as a signal that asset quality has strengthened. However, a recent Seeking Alpha article showed that while credit card delinquencies held steady for February, charge-offs have risen from 3.53% in January to 3.83%. While credit cards are just a part of Bank of America's business, this does show that financial health in the economy may be on a weakening trajectory. With rate hikes this year no longer being an impossibility, there is a risk that charge offs could edge up for the firm.

Other Considerations

Moving forward, there are some other developments that are noteworthy. Firstly, back in February, it was reported that the Fed is proposing rules to allow big banks to get more involved with the mortgage industry again. As Yoel Minkoff of Seeking Alpha writes, this would...

...encourage Wall Street institutions like Wells Fargo (WFC), Bank of America (BAC), and JPMorgan Chase (JPM) to claw back their share, and compete more aggressively for the mortgages typically sold to or guaranteed by government-sponsored agencies like Fannie Mae (FNMA) and Freddie Mac (FMCC).

In my view, if this shift in policy and regulation does happen, Bank of America could see a meaningful increase in their overall business activity. At the end of the day, the mortgage industry is one that is massive and provides a lot of opportunity, and so over the long run, the big banks could benefit.

For 2026 Q1 specifically, Bank of America's Co-President Dean Athanasia gave some early updates on March 10th. While Q1 isn't over yet, he stated that investment banking revenues were up around "10%, double digits" compared to this time last year and that wealth management "fees are up double digits." For me, these early numbers sound quite robust, and so Bank of America seems likely to report strong results for Q1 in April.

Valuation Decently Attractive

To start off 2026, Bank of America's P/B ratio has taken a rather steep slide. At the current reading of 1.21, the multiple is slightly above its three year median while being just a hair above the five year median. In my view, this offers investors a decent amount of opportunity right now.

Today, it was shown that Bank of America has remained resilient. Growth was seen in all segments for Q4, and the company has controlled their expenses well to boost profitability. Looking forward to Q1 and 2026 in general, I would say that the firm is well positioned. Guidance is respectable, and the current rate environment seems to be favorable for their performance. While there are obviously geopolitical risks that could slow the global economy, Bank of America has a decent amount of opportunity moving forward in areas like private credit products and perhaps even mortgages further down the road. Lastly, as stated above, early numbers for Q1 sound bullish, and so as a whole, I view the outlook as positive despite what's happening in the world today.

While an increase in cautiousness in this environment is justified, I would say that the P/B ratio's sharp contraction to start this year is a bit of an overreaction. Bank of America's ROE and ROTCE continue to rise, and so investors should actually be happy with what they're seeing. With the multiple now basically at the 5-year median, I believe the risk/reward remains attractive.

Takeaway

With private credit concerns causing flashbacks of 2008 and the war in the Middle East fueling worries about inflation and economic growth around the world, it is not a huge surprise that Bank of America hasn't performed too well so far this year. However, when looking at the fundamentals and the opportunities moving forward, I would say that the valuation is in a position now where the attractiveness of the stock remains strong. With the environment creating potentially excessive amounts of fear, I believe this is a good chance to buy the dip in Bank of America stock.

data.title: Growth Signals Remain Intact Despite Geopolitical Shocks
data.description:
Geopolitics keeps markets on edge

The conflict in the Middle East remained a key driver of market sentiment this week, with rapidly shifting headlines contributing to heightened volatility.

Early in the week, reports of a partial ceasefire and constructive talks between the U.S. and Iran supported a rally in financial markets. However, that optimism faded as conflicting reports emerged midweek, including denials of negotiations and signs of increased military activity. By Thursday, renewed indications of progress and a potential extension of the ceasefire once again lifted sentiment.

Despite these swings, market moves were net flat; through Thursday’s close, both global equity markets and sovereign bond yields were little changed on the week.

This pattern underscores how sensitive markets remain to geopolitical developments, particularly around the potential duration and impact of any disruption to energy supply.

Growth remains modest but stable

Away from geopolitics, incoming economic data pointed to continued, albeit modest, growth across advanced economies. Global purchasing managers’ indices (PMIs) for March remained above 50 across both manufacturing and services sectors, indicating ongoing expansion.

In the U.S., additional detail from regional Federal Reserve manufacturing surveys suggests a potential pickup in business investment. Measures of capital expenditure intentions have shown signs of improvement, pointing not only to continued support from AI-related investment, but also to a possible broadening in spending.

Policy may be playing a role. Business investment could be benefiting from expensing provisions introduced in last year’s fiscal legislation, which were designed to support capital spending.

Consumer resilience holds, for now

Another key focus this week has been the U.S. consumer, particularly in light of higher gasoline prices tied to recent energy market volatility. So far, the data points to continued resilience.

High-frequency indicators, including credit card spending data and weekly retail sales measures, suggest that consumer activity remained solid through the first three weeks of March. Despite rising fuel costs and geopolitical uncertainty, spending has held up.

That resilience will be important to monitor in the weeks ahead. Sustained pressure from higher energy prices could eventually weigh on consumption, but current data suggests the consumer remains a source of support for the U.S. economy.

Looking forward, next week brings several key macroeconomic releases, including ISM business surveys and the U.S. nonfarm payrolls report, which will provide further insight into the strength of economic momentum.

These views are subject to change at any time based upon market or other conditions and are current as of the date at the top of the page. data.title: Summit Hotel Properties: Undervalued With High Yield Make This A Buy
data.description:
Introduction

I have written about Summit Hotel Properties (INN) several times previously, most recently in June 2025. Back then, I rated the company as a “Buy” due to its healthy balance sheet and high dividend yield. With the release of the company’s Q4 2025 earnings last month, it seems like a good time for me to re-evaluate the company to see if my rating remains the same.

Business Model & FY 2025 Performance

Summit Hotel Properties operates 95 hotels with over 14,000 guestrooms in 42 markets across 24 states. It is a select-service hospitality REIT, meaning its properties are rooms-focused. By concentrating on the core aspect of rooms, the company is able to reduce capital requirements, which in turn results in a leaner cost structure and ultimately higher operating margins.

The majority of the company’s hotels are located in the top 50 US markets. Crucially, the hotels are well-diversified geographically. Subsequent to year-end, in February, the company sold the 122-guestroom Hilton Garden Inn in Longview, Texas, bringing its portfolio down to 94 properties.

Historically, the company has a track record of outperformance, even compared to its select-service peers. This is in no small part due to its operational platform, which leverages the company’s expertise and experience in revenue and asset management, design and construction, and capital allocation, all underpinned by robust business intelligence and data analytics.

For FY 2025, Summit Hotel Properties generated an EBITDA margin of 33.4%, outperforming its select-service peers by 130 basis points (32.1%) and its full-service peers by a huge 630 basis points (27.1%). This came despite a difficult year, which saw RevPAR fall by 2.2% and AFFO falling from $0.96/share in 2024 to $0.85/share in 2025. The difficult year was mainly attributed to a reduction in United States government-related travel (historically comprising 15% to 20% of room nights) and a decline in international travel to the United States. The situation was further exacerbated in Q4 2025 by a prolonged government shutdown, which further affected demand.

Despite all this, however, there were signs of improvement in Q4 2025 alone. RevPAR trends improved by 240 basis points from Q3. Perhaps most importantly, the RevPAR index increased by nearly 220 basis points in Q4 2025 to 117, which means the company’s hotels generate 17% more revenue per available room than their competitive sets. This bodes well for the company, demonstrating that the company is still able to gain market share despite a challenging macroenvironment.

Balance Sheet

One of the most compelling aspects of the company lies in the strength of its balance sheet, which has been substantially de-risked over the past year through proactive management action. In February 2026, the company drew on its $275 million delayed draw term loan and its corporate revolver to fully repay its outstanding $287.5 million of convertible notes due the same month. Subsequently, the company has no debt maturities until 2028, with an average debt maturity of 3.4 years.

The company has a total outstanding debt of $1.1 billion, of which 77% carries fixed interest rates after swap agreements. This reduces interest rate risk and provides certainty for the company, an important factor during periods of uncertainty. The company’s total liquidity currently stands at approximately $240 million, more than half its market capitalization, comprising both cash and cash equivalents as well as its revolving credit facility. This gives the company the flexibility to act on opportunistic acquisitions, as well as weather any revenue volatility.

The company has a fixed charge coverage of 2.2x, showcasing its capability to service its capital structure even in uncertain times.

All in all, the company has a strong balance sheet, with no maturities due in the short-term, which provides the company with significant flexibility.

Dividend

As always, any article on a REIT would be remiss without mentioning its dividends, and this is where Summit Hotel Properties shines. The company currently pays a quarterly dividend of $0.08/share, and has maintained this for the past 2 years now. Based on the latest share price of $4.29/share, an annual dividend of $0.32/share gives the company a high dividend yield of 7.46%.

Most importantly, when considering the midpoint of the 2026 guidance ($0.79/share), the company has a dividend payout ratio of around 40%, a very comfortable figure. This indicates that the company’s dividends are well-covered and sustainable, even if the macroeconomic environment may be challenging.

Valuation

Summit Hotel Properties, by almost any metric, is deeply discounted at current prices. Based on the latest share price of $4.29/share, the company is currently trading at just 5.1x 2025 AFFO and approximately 5.4x the midpoint of the 2026 AFFO guidance, both of which are below both the average of its peers and the historical average. Assuming a multiple of just 10x would see the share price jump to $7.90/share, an 84% upside.

A look at the company’s price-to-book ratio (P/B ratio) tells a similar story. The company is currently trading at a P/B ratio of 0.55, just over half the value of its net asset value (Source: https://www.morningstar.com/stocks/xnys/inn/valuation). Such discounts are typically associated with companies in financial distress and not a company such as Summit Hotel Properties.

A look at the consensus average price target from analysts supports my view that the company is undervalued at present. Despite 3 analysts recommending a “Hold”, the average price target for the company is $5.38/share, a 25% increase on the current share price.

Catalysts and Risks

The main catalyst for Summit Hotel Properties comes in the form of the 2026 FIFA World Cup, with the company uniquely positioned to capture this demand, with 44 of its hotels located in six US host markets, which will collectively host 44 matches. With hotel booking volumes in World Cup host cities rising sharply, the company stands to benefit from this increase in demand due to its high proportion of hotels in the area. Earlier this year, CEO Jonathan Stanner said as much in an interview.

On the flip side, there are several risks to consider before rushing to invest in the company. Firstly, the company is exposed to the macroeconomic environment, and a recession in the economy would undoubtedly affect the company. While the company has performed commendably so far, a prolonged recession would potentially drive RevPAR below the 2025 levels.

Next, if the government travel restrictions or budget reductions become permanent instead of cyclical, the company would have to make up for the fall in demand from other demand segments. This would undoubtedly be challenging for the company, and there is no guarantee they will be able to fully replace the demand.

Conclusion

The company is currently paying a high dividend (7.46%), which is amply covered by a conservative dividend payout ratio of around 40%. The company has no maturities due for the next 2 years, until 2028, and the upcoming 2026 FIFA World Cup is expected to significantly increase demand for the company’s hotels. Despite all this, the company is trading at a P/B ratio of 0.55x and at just an AFFO multiple of 5.4x, implying that it is undervalued at present. I believe this undervaluation is due in no small part to the market pricing in the uncertainty in the macroeconomic environment at present and presents a potential buying opportunity for investors, providing a margin of safety. For all of the above reasons, I rate the company a “Buy”.

data.title: I Was So Wrong About Upstart: Do Not Ignore The AI Threat
data.description:
AI has sent growth stocks a bout of extreme volatility. Upstart (UPST) has been caught in the crossfire, in spite of continuing to post solid growth rates. But rising macro uncertainty as well as the potential for AI-led competition has changed the way I look at the same numbers. I was disappointed by the pause in margin expansion and find the current valuation stretched even after the recent fall. I am downgrading the stock to a sell rating.

UPST Stock Price

I last covered UPST in November, where I upgraded the stock to a buy rating on account of the volatility. That has proven true early as the stock has fallen yet another 36%.

I typically loathe downgrading stocks after a significant drawdown, but the setup here means everything.

UPST Stock Key Metrics

UPST is a financial tech company that uses artificial intelligence to underwrite loans. This is an AI-first company that has improved its underwriting models based on cycles even before the rise of generative AI. Ironically, the stock might be more popular if it had come public after the rise of AI, but here we are.

In the most recent quarter, UPST generated 35% YoY revenue growth to $296 million, slightly exceeding guidance of $288 million. I suspect investors were disappointed by both the steep deceleration in YoY growth (which is understandable due to tough comps) as well as the smaller magnitude of the beat to guidance.

The company saw contribution margins contract 400 bps QoQ and 800 bps YoY to 53%, in line with guidance.

The company generated $64 million in adjusted EBITDA, exceeding guidance of $63 million. Similar to the top line, I viewed the small beat with disappointment and also note that margins retreated 400 bps QoQ after increasing sequentially to that 26% level in the third quarter.

The company generated $19 million in GAAP net income, exceeding guidance of $17 million but also representing a sharp QoQ contraction in margins.

Management notes that their loan originations have been performing within their targeted ranges (they had briefly underperformed a couple of years ago amidst the rapid rise in interest rates), with their products still offering an attractive yield relative to the alternatives.

The company saw its loans held on the balance sheet decline by 20% QoQ, but the decline was mostly in those categorized as R&D. I viewed this development with mixed feelings, as I would have thought the "personal loans" would have been a higher priority to off-load.

Looking ahead, management guided the 2026 year to see around $1.4 billion in revenue, representing 34.6% YoY growth. Management targeted a 21% adjusted EBITDA margin, which I found disappointing, as I was hoping for more margin expansion. The company has undergone a CEO transition in which Co-founder and previous CTO Paul Gu would become the new CEO. Management gave 3-year guidance of 35% annual revenue growth with adjusted EBITDA margins ending up at 25%.

I found that guidance to be puzzling. I have already noted that investors (or at least I) were hoping for a stronger commitment to margin expansion. But this seems to be the kind of business that is highly exposed to fluctuations in the economy, which aren't in management's control. I have concerns that issuing multi-year guidance (which I also note implies no deceleration in top-line growth) may be setting up the company for potential disappointment, as the stock is unlikely to benefit from higher expectations. On the conference call, management noted that the fact that contribution margins have gone down is "expected and intentional" due to the company having "grown rapidly in secured products and prime borrowers, segments that come with lower take rates but dramatically larger market sizes." This explanation is reasonable, but I still am of the view that operating margins need to rise, as a leaner cost structure could help insulate the company in the event of market volatility. With many tech stocks crumbling left and right due to fears of potential disruption from generative AI, management noted that they are insulated due to their "proprietary algorithms that we've built over many years." I am less certain, as I think at the very least generative AI may lead to the rise of more competition.

Is UPST Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

As of recent prices, UPST traded at just 12x earnings. I note that this is non-GAAP earnings, though. Inclusive of stock-based compensation, I estimate that UPST is trading at around 30x forward earnings (I am assuming that SBC remains at around $130 million, as it has for the last two years).

Consensus estimates call for the company to sustain robust top-line growth over the next 3 years, though falling short of management's guidance of 35% CAGR.

The stock is materially cheaper than the last time I looked at it when I called it a buy. But now, I am less bullish. What changed? The macro environment appears to be showing concerns that AI may lead to widespread job displacements, which in turn might lead to a weakening economy. Against that outlook, companies like UPST might see pressure to both target returns and demand for loans. The rise of generative AI and vibe coding has (from the outside looking in) reduced the barriers to entry for competitors, as they may be able to develop far faster than before. I am skeptical with regard to management's claim that their proprietary models insulate them from AI pressures, but even if we ignore that, it is hard to ignore the potential reality that the real bull case has been dismantled. At least to me, the long-term bull thesis for the stock has been for banks across the country (and world) to use UPST's services to help mass-underwrite loans. This would clearly make UPST an asset-light business, and the great scale would mean strong margins.

Vibe coding throws a wrench in those plans: I now expect every bank, big or small (but especially the big ones), to be prioritizing building their own AI-powered lending models (if they haven't already done so). It's not just the future outlook that has lessened my optimism. As I discussed in the prior section, the company has seen gains to margins come to a pause. With the long-term growth story potentially in question, I expect more focus to come on profit margins. Management's guidance seems to indicate that the company is undergoing a period of growth as it enters new, lower-margin verticals to capture market share. This kind of strategy might have made sense a few months ago when I did not retain the same pessimism regarding the macro setup. I have already mentioned the potential risks of issuing aggressive multi-year guidance, but that honestly pales in comparison to the above points.

Assuming the company reaches 20% net margins, the stock trades at around 9x earnings. If one believes in the growth story, then that valuation looks at least reasonable. But given the escalating competition and increased uncertainty with regard to the path to margin expansion, I no longer view that multiple as being cheap enough for the level of risk. I see fair value as being more in the 4x to 6x earnings range, which would offer greater potential reward for the risk. My fair value range would rise if management can deliver on sustained margin expansion.

UPST Stock Conclusion

It feels like I have seen this story before. UPST was a big winner in my portfolio coming out of the pandemic but ended up disappointing in 2022 as its models struggled to calibrate for the market volatility. The company does indeed look much different than it did then, but we are now facing a new set of unprecedented risks from AI, ranging from potential high unemployment to the rise of new competition. The stock valuation is pricing in a lot of growth, especially considering that GAAP margins are not yet at mature levels. I am downgrading the stock to a sell rating.
